news_summary: Recent advancements reveal groundbreaking insights into space-time concepts, innovative materials for lunar exploration, surprising origins of water on Mercury, and detailed observations of distant planetary atmospheres.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes apportent des éclairages révolutionnaires sur les concepts d'espace-temps, les matériaux innovants destinés à l'exploration lunaire, les origines surprenantes de l'eau sur Mercure et les observations détaillées des atmosphères planétaires lointaines.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances aportan conocimientos revolucionarios sobre los conceptos del espacio-tiempo, materiales innovadores para la exploración lunar, los sorprendentes orígenes del agua en Mercurio y observaciones detalladas de las atmósferas de planetas lejanos.

articles.title: Einstein’s “wormhole” may actually reveal a hidden mirror of time
articles.summary: What if wormholes were never cosmic tunnels at all? New research suggests Einstein and Rosen’s famous “bridge” may actually reveal something even stranger: time itself could flow in two directions at once. Instead of connecting distant places in space, these bridges may connect mirror versions of time deep inside quantum physics, potentially solving the long-standing black hole information paradox and hinting that our universe existed before the Big Bang.
articles.content:
Wormholes are often imagined as tunnels through space or time — shortcuts across the universe. But this image rests on a misunderstanding of work by physicists Albert Einstein and Nathan Rosen.

In 1935, while studying the behavior of particles in regions of extreme gravity, Einstein and Rosen introduced what they called a “bridge”: a mathematical link between two perfectly symmetrical copies of spacetime. It was not intended as a passage for travel, but as a way to maintain consistency between gravity and quantum physics. Only later did Einstein–Rosen bridges become associated with wormholes, despite having little to do with the original idea.

But in new research, my colleagues and I show that the original Einstein–Rosen bridge points to something far stranger — and more fundamental — than a wormhole.

The puzzle Einstein and Rosen were addressing was never about space travel, but about how quantum fields behave in curved spacetime. Interpreted this way, the Einstein–Rosen bridge acts as a mirror in spacetime: a connection between two microscopic arrows of time.

Quantum mechanics governs nature at the smallest scales such as particles, while Einstein’s theory of general relativity applies to gravity and spacetime. Reconciling the two remains one of physics’ deepest challenges. And excitingly, our reinterpretation may offer a path to doing this.

A misunderstood legacy

The “wormhole” interpretation emerged decades after Einstein and Rosen’s work, when physicists speculated about crossing from one side of spacetime to the other, most notably in the late-1980s research.

But those same analyses also made clear how speculative the idea was: within general relativity, such a journey is forbidden. The bridge pinches off faster than light could traverse it, rendering it non-traversable. Einstein–Rosen bridges are therefore unstable and unobservable — mathematical structures, not portals.

Nevertheless, the wormhole metaphor flourished in popular culture and speculative theoretical physics. The idea that black holes might connect distant regions of the cosmos — or even act as time machines — inspired countless papers, books and films.

Yet there is no observational evidence for macroscopic wormholes, nor any compelling theoretical reason to expect them within Einstein’s theory. While speculative extensions of physics — such as exotic forms of matter or modifications of general relativity — have been proposed to support such structures, they remain untested and highly conjectural.

Two arrows of time

Our recent work revisits the Einstein–Rosen bridge puzzle using a modern quantum interpretation of time, building on ideas developed by Sravan Kumar and João Marto.

Most fundamental laws of physics do not distinguish between past and future, or between left and right. If time or space is reversed in their equations, the laws remain valid. Taking these symmetries seriously leads to a different interpretation of the Einstein–Rosen bridge.

Rather than a tunnel through space, it can be understood as two complementary components of a quantum state. In one, time flows forward; in the other, it flows backward from its mirror-reflected position.

This symmetry is not a philosophical preference. Once infinities are excluded, quantum evolution must remain complete and reversible at the microscopic level — even in the presence of gravity.

The “bridge” expresses the fact that both time components are needed to describe a complete physical system. In ordinary situations, physicists ignore the time-reversed component by choosing a single arrow of time.

But near black holes, or in expanding and collapsing universes, both directions must be included for a consistent quantum description. It is here that Einstein–Rosen bridges naturally arise.

Solving the information paradox

At the microscopic level, the bridge allows information to pass across what appears to us as an event horizon – a point of no return. Information does not vanish; it continues evolving, but along the opposite, mirror temporal direction.

This framework offers a natural resolution to the famous black hole information paradox. In 1974, Stephen Hawking showed that black holes radiate heat and can eventually evaporate, apparently erasing all information about what fell into them — contradicting the quantum principle that evolution must preserve information.

The paradox arises only if we insist on describing horizons using a single, one-sided arrow of time extrapolated to infinity — an assumption quantum mechanics itself does not require.

If the full quantum description includes both time directions, nothing is truly lost. Information leaves our time direction and re-emerges along the reversed one. Completeness and causality are preserved, without invoking exotic new physics.

These ideas are difficult to grasp because we are macroscopic beings who experience only one direction of time. On everyday scales, disorder — or entropy — tends to increase. A highly ordered state naturally evolves into a disordered one, never the reverse. This gives us an arrow of time.

But quantum mechanics allows more subtle behavior. Intriguingly, evidence for this hidden structure may already exist. The cosmic microwave background — the afterglow of the Big Bang — shows a small but persistent asymmetry: a preference for one spatial orientation over its mirror image.

This anomaly has puzzled cosmologists for two decades. Standard models assign it extremely low probability — unless mirror quantum components are included.

Echoes of a prior universe?

This picture connects naturally to a deeper possibility. What we call the “Big Bang” may not have been the absolute beginning, but a bounce — a quantum transition between two time-reversed phases of cosmic evolution.

In such a scenario, black holes could act as bridges not just between time directions, but between different cosmological epochs. Our universe might be the interior of a black hole formed in another, parent cosmos. This could have formed as a closed region of spacetime collapsed, bounced back and began expanding as the universe we observe today.

If this picture is correct, it also offers a way for observations to decide. Relics from the pre-bounce phase — such as smaller black holes — could survive the transition and reappear in our expanding universe. Some of the unseen matter we attribute to dark matter could, in fact, be made of such relics.

In this view, the Big Bang evolved from conditions in a preceding contraction. Wormholes aren’t necessary: the bridge is temporal, not spatial — and the Big Bang becomes a gateway, not a beginning.

This reinterpretation of Einstein–Rosen bridges offers no shortcuts across galaxies, no time travel and no science-fiction wormholes or hyperspace. What it offers is far deeper. It offers a consistent quantum picture of gravity in which spacetime embodies a balance between opposite directions of time — and where our universe may have had a history before the Big Bang.

It does not overthrow Einstein’s relativity or quantum physics — it completes them. The next revolution in physics may not take us faster than light — but it could reveal that time, deep down in the microscopic world and in a bouncing universe, flows both ways.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant scientific breakthrough in theoretical physics, offering a novel interpretation of Einstein–Rosen bridges that could resolve the black hole information paradox and suggest our universe existed before the Big Bang. This has broad implications for fundamental physics and cosmology, providing a hopeful and inspiring advancement in understanding the universe.
articles.personality_title: New research suggests time flows both ways through Einstein’s ‘bridge’
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Physicists Albert Einstein and Nathan Rosen introduced the idea of a “bridge” in 1935 to explain how particles behave in strong gravity. Later, this idea was linked to wormholes, imagined as tunnels through space or time. However, wormholes as space shortcuts do not fit Einstein’s original concept.

**What happened** – New research reinterprets the Einstein–Rosen bridge not as a tunnel in space but as a connection between two directions of time. In this view, time flows forward in one part and backward in the other, forming a mirror-like link at the quantum level. This idea helps explain how information can pass through black holes without being lost.

**Impact** – This discovery offers a new way to solve the black hole information paradox, a puzzle about whether information disappears inside black holes. It also suggests the universe may have existed before the Big Bang, with the Big Bang acting as a “bounce” between two time directions rather than a beginning. This challenges common ideas about time and space and could change how scientists understand the universe’s history.

**What's next step** – Scientists will look for evidence of this time-mirror idea in cosmic observations, such as leftover black holes from before the Big Bang or patterns in the cosmic microwave background radiation. If confirmed, this could reshape theories about the universe’s origin and the nature of time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists propose that Einstein’s bridge links two directions of time, offering a new way to understand black holes and the universe’s history before the Big Bang.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle recherche suggère que le temps s’écoule dans les deux sens à travers le « pont » d’Einstein
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les physiciens Albert Einstein et Nathan Rosen ont introduit en 1935 l’idée d’un « pont » pour expliquer le comportement des particules dans une forte gravité. Plus tard, cette idée a été associée aux trous de ver, imaginés comme des tunnels à travers l’espace ou le temps. Cependant, les trous de ver comme raccourcis spatiaux ne correspondent pas au concept original d’Einstein.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle recherche réinterprète le pont Einstein–Rosen non pas comme un tunnel dans l’espace, mais comme une connexion entre deux directions du temps. Dans cette vision, le temps s’écoule vers l’avant dans une partie et vers l’arrière dans l’autre, formant un lien miroir au niveau quantique. Cette idée aide à expliquer comment l’information peut traverser les trous noirs sans disparaître.

**Impact** – Cette découverte offre une nouvelle manière de résoudre le paradoxe de l’information dans les trous noirs, une énigme sur la disparition possible de l’information à l’intérieur des trous noirs. Elle suggère aussi que l’univers aurait pu exister avant le Big Bang, celui-ci agissant comme un « rebond » entre deux directions du temps plutôt que comme un commencement. Cela remet en question les idées courantes sur le temps et l’espace et pourrait changer la compréhension de l’histoire de l’univers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques chercheront des preuves de cette idée du miroir temporel dans les observations cosmiques, comme des trous noirs anciens ou des motifs dans le rayonnement fossile du Big Bang. Si cela est confirmé, cela pourrait transformer les théories sur l’origine de l’univers et la nature du temps.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les chercheurs proposent que le pont d’Einstein relie deux directions du temps, offrant une nouvelle façon de comprendre les trous noirs et l’histoire de l’univers avant le Big Bang.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación sugiere que el tiempo fluye en dos direcciones a través del ‘puente’ de Einstein
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los físicos Albert Einstein y Nathan Rosen introdujeron en 1935 la idea de un “puente” para explicar cómo se comportan las partículas en gravedad fuerte. Más tarde, esta idea se relacionó con los agujeros de gusano, imaginados como túneles a través del espacio o el tiempo. Sin embargo, los agujeros de gusano como atajos espaciales no encajan con el concepto original de Einstein.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva investigación reinterpreta el puente Einstein–Rosen no como un túnel en el espacio, sino como una conexión entre dos direcciones del tiempo. En esta visión, el tiempo fluye hacia adelante en una parte y hacia atrás en la otra, formando un enlace espejo a nivel cuántico. Esta idea ayuda a explicar cómo la información puede pasar por los agujeros negros sin perderse.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento ofrece una nueva forma de resolver la paradoja de la información en los agujeros negros, un problema sobre si la información desaparece dentro de ellos. También sugiere que el universo pudo existir antes del Big Bang, con el Big Bang actuando como un “rebote” entre dos direcciones del tiempo en lugar de un comienzo. Esto desafía las ideas comunes sobre el tiempo y el espacio y podría cambiar cómo los científicos entienden la historia del universo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos buscarán evidencia de esta idea del espejo temporal en observaciones cósmicas, como agujeros negros antiguos o patrones en la radiación cósmica de fondo. Si se confirma, podría cambiar las teorías sobre el origen del universo y la naturaleza del tiempo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos proponen que el puente de Einstein conecta dos direcciones del tiempo, ofreciendo una nueva forma de entender los agujeros negros y la historia del universo antes del Big Bang.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two intertwined, mirror-like spirals of light representing opposing arrows of time, connected by a glowing, bridge-shaped quantum structure hovering above a softly glowing cosmic horizon that hints at a bouncing universe, with subtle reflections suggesting a hidden mirror of time within curved spacetime.

articles.title: New Material Could Help NASA Melt Moon Rocks, Harness Lunar Resources
articles.summary: A material recently discovered and tested at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland could help astronauts pack lighter for future missions to the Moon. NASA is researching ways explorers could “live off the land” by harnessing lunar resources, including melting Moon rocks to extract metals for building infrastructure and oxygen for fuel and life support. […]
articles.content:
Researchers Dr. Kevin Yu, left, and Dr. Jamesa Stokes prepare to remove a sample of a new material they discovered from a furnace inside a laboratory at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland in October 2024. Quenching, or bringing the temperature of the sample down as quickly as possible, helps to ensure no more reactions occur as the sample cools so scientists can focus on studying how it behaves at high temperatures. NASA/Jef Janis

A material recently discovered and tested at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland could help astronauts pack lighter for future missions to the Moon. NASA is researching ways explorers could “live off the land” by harnessing lunar resources, including melting Moon rocks to extract metals for building infrastructure and oxygen for fuel and life support.

As part of a graduate fellowship through the agency’s Space Technology Graduate Research Opportunities, Dr. Kevin Yu, who now works as a technologist at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, teamed up with Dr. Jamesa Stokes, a materials research engineer at NASA Glenn, to study how a variety of substances interacted with liquefied Moon dust.

You could call it lava, because it’s basically rocks that are crushed up and then melted. It’s very corrosive, and it will very quickly eat through a lot of commonly used refractory, or heat-resistant, materials. Dr. kevin yu Technologist at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory

About six months into their research, Stokes and Yu realized they’d stumbled across something promising and entirely new. After combining simulated lunar dust with a compound called scandium oxide and heat treating the mixture using a red-hot furnace, they discovered that an unknown material had formed. The researchers checked and double-checked their work, but the material didn’t match any of the more than 1 million substances in their X-ray analysis database.

A sample of the new material researchers discovered at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland sits inside a platinum crucible, or heat-resistant container, after being removed from a high-temperature furnace. Behind the silver-colored container is a dome that protects the sample during handling. NASA/Jef Janis

Nothing about the material had ever been studied before, so the team started from scratch, measuring the substance’s chemical composition. To make small, isolated samples and continue testing how it reacted with molten Moon dust, they used special grinding and mixing equipment in their laboratory to crush up around eight basic oxide components in ethyl alcohol before baking the mixture at more than 2,900 degrees Fahrenheit inside the furnace.

“It’s actually a very cool-looking powder; it goes in pink, almost like strawberry milk,” Yu said. “It has a built-in color indicator, so by the time you’re done with it, it turns to a light beige or tan color, and that’s how you know the reaction has proceeded the way you wanted it to.”

The pink powder shown at the far right is used to make the new material researchers discovered at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland. The other powders to the left are two types of simulated Moon dirt used to represent dust from both the brighter regions of its surface (referred to as lunar highlands) and the darker regions (referred to as lunar maria). NASA/Jef Janis

After analyzing their results, the team found that the new substance isn’t corroded too quickly by the molten Moon dirt and can withstand the high temperatures needed to melt it — up to six times hotter than the oven in your kitchen. While it’s made with scandium oxide, which can be expensive, it costs much less than precious metals like platinum that would normally be used in these types of high-temperature processes.

The researchers’ insights could help influence NASA’s designs for a future technology that would extract resources from Moon rocks, and the new material could be used to make the pipes or basins holding molten dust inside this potential technology.

The new material’s characteristics also could prove ideal for making coatings that protect parts inside of jet engines, which can reach similarly scorching temperatures. The researchers found it is lighter, less dense, and better at insulating heat than current state-of-the-art coating materials.

Researchers Dr. Jamesa Stokes, left, and Dr. Kevin Yu pose for a portrait inside of a laboratory at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland in October 2024. NASA/Jef Janis

While Yu and Stokes have now completed their initial tests, they hope to fine-tune the material in the future to purify it and make it even more affordable to produce. Materials research will be integral to exploring the harsh environments of the Moon and beyond.

You can have the best idea in the world for a structure or a vehicle, but if you don’t have the materials that have the right properties to make your vision come true, it’s not going to succeed no matter how well you design it. Dr. Jamesa stokes Materials Research Engineer at NASA Glenn

Studying new materials also advances NASA’s work on Earth.

“I think trying to push what’s possible with materials also allows for a lot of breakthroughs on the terrestrial side. Having a better understanding of materials for all sorts of applications is what gets me excited to go to work in the morning,” Yu said. “That’s why I love NASA’s mission; it’s for the benefit of all.”

This materials research is supported by NASA’s Space Technology Mission Directorate and NASA’s Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate.

For more information, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a new material that can withstand extreme temperatures and corrosive lunar dust, enabling future lunar resource extraction and infrastructure building. This breakthrough has broad implications for sustainable space exploration and could also benefit terrestrial technologies, demonstrating a positive real-world impact with substantial scope and detailed context.
articles.personality_title: NASA discovers new heat-resistant material to help melt Moon rocks for future missions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on ways for astronauts to use materials found on the Moon, like melting Moon rocks to get metals and oxygen. This would help build structures and provide fuel and air, making space missions lighter and more efficient.

**What happened** – In October 2024, researchers at NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland found a new material while testing how substances react with melted Moon dust. Dr. Kevin Yu and Dr. Jamesa Stokes combined simulated lunar dust with scandium oxide and heated it above 2,900°F. This created a material that had never been seen before.

**Impact** – This new material can handle very high heat and resist corrosion from molten Moon dust better than many existing materials. It could be used to build parts like pipes or containers to hold melted Moon rocks during resource extraction. It is also lighter and better at insulating heat than current materials used in jet engines. This discovery could make future Moon missions safer and more practical.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to improve the material by making it purer and cheaper to produce. Their ongoing work will help design new tools and machines for living and working on the Moon and could lead to advances in technology on Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s discovery of a new heat-resistant material could make it easier to use Moon resources and improve technologies both in space and on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA découvre un nouveau matériau résistant à la chaleur pour fondre les roches lunaires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA cherche des moyens pour que les astronautes utilisent les ressources de la Lune, comme fondre les roches lunaires pour extraire des métaux et de l’oxygène. Cela aiderait à construire des infrastructures et fournirait carburant et air, rendant les missions plus légères et efficaces.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En octobre 2024, des chercheurs du Centre de recherche Glenn de la NASA à Cleveland ont découvert un nouveau matériau en testant la réaction de substances avec de la poussière lunaire fondue. Le Dr Kevin Yu et le Dr Jamesa Stokes ont combiné de la poussière lunaire simulée avec de l’oxyde de scandium et l’ont chauffée à plus de 1 600 °C. Cela a créé un matériau jamais vu auparavant.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau matériau supporte des températures très élevées et résiste mieux à la corrosion causée par la poussière lunaire fondue que beaucoup d’autres matériaux. Il pourrait servir à fabriquer des pièces comme des tuyaux ou des récipients pour contenir la poussière fondue lors de l’extraction des ressources. Il est aussi plus léger et meilleur isolant thermique que les matériaux actuels utilisés dans les moteurs d’avion. Cette découverte pourrait rendre les futures missions lunaires plus sûres et pratiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer le matériau en le purifiant et en réduisant son coût de fabrication. Leur travail aidera à concevoir de nouveaux outils et machines pour vivre et travailler sur la Lune et pourrait aussi améliorer les technologies sur Terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte par la NASA d’un nouveau matériau résistant à la chaleur pourrait faciliter l’utilisation des ressources lunaires et améliorer des technologies dans l’espace et sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA descubre nuevo material resistente al calor para fundir rocas lunares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA busca formas para que los astronautas usen recursos de la Luna, como fundir rocas lunares para obtener metales y oxígeno. Esto ayudaría a construir infraestructuras y a proveer combustible y aire, haciendo las misiones más ligeras y eficientes.

Qué pasó** – En octubre de 2024, investigadores del Centro de Investigación Glenn de la NASA en Cleveland encontraron un nuevo material al probar cómo reaccionan sustancias con polvo lunar fundido. El Dr. Kevin Yu y la Dra. Jamesa Stokes combinaron polvo lunar simulado con óxido de escandio y lo calentaron a más de 1,600 °C. Esto creó un material nunca antes visto.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo material puede soportar temperaturas muy altas y resiste la corrosión causada por el polvo lunar fundido mejor que muchos otros materiales. Podría usarse para fabricar partes como tuberías o contenedores para sostener el polvo fundido durante la extracción de recursos. También es más liviano y mejor aislante térmico que los materiales actuales usados en motores de avión. Este descubrimiento podría hacer las futuras misiones lunares más seguras y prácticas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean mejorar el material purificándolo y haciéndolo más barato de producir. Su trabajo ayudará a diseñar nuevas herramientas y máquinas para vivir y trabajar en la Luna y podría llevar a avances tecnológicos en la Tierra.

**Resumen en una frase** – El descubrimiento de la NASA de un nuevo material resistente al calor podría facilitar el uso de recursos lunares y mejorar tecnologías tanto en el espacio como en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing furnace surrounded by softly illuminated crucibles filled with softly glowing pink and beige powders, set against a backdrop of abstract lunar rock textures and gentle silhouettes of scientific tools symbolizing discovery and innovation in lunar materials research.

articles.title: Mercury may have gained all of its unexpected water in a single day
articles.summary: Despite being the closest planet to the sun, Mercury has thick deposits of ice at its poles, and now we may understand the events that formed them over just one Mercurian day
articles.content:
Despite being the closest planet to the sun, Mercury has thick deposits of ice at its poles, and now we may understand the events that formed them over just one Mercurian day

We may now know how Mercury gained its ice deposits NASA’s Scientific Visualization Studio

Around 100 million years ago, the surface of Mercury suddenly underwent a dramatic change. Before then, its surface was relatively dry and ice-free – not surprising, as daytime temperatures there can reach upwards of 430°C (806°F) – but over the course of a single Mercurian day, all that changed.

The poles of Mercury are home to craters whose bottoms never see sunlight, known as permanently shadowed regions. Thanks to NASA’s Messenger spacecraft, which orbited Mercury between 2011 and 2015, we know that those craters contain deposits of ice several metres deep. But how that ice got there is puzzling.

Previous research has suggested that it may have been brought there by a comet-like body around 17 kilometres across that smashed into Mercury at a speed of about 30 kilometres per second. Now, new simulations from Parvathy Prem at the Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory in Maryland and her colleagues suggest that it may have been a larger, slower collision.

“We’ve known for a while that Mercury’s poles have ice. The idea that those ice deposits might have been laid down by an impactor is also not new, but this is the first time we’ve really modelled that process and visualised what might have gone on from the start to the end,” says Prem. “It’s the first time we’ve looked in detail [at] how exactly the movie plays out.”

That movie starts with a huge chunk of ice and rock slamming into Mercury, creating the enormous Hokusai crater that we see on the planet’s surface today. As the impactor hit the ground, it would have vapourised almost completely, leaving Mercury with an extremely tenuous, but water-rich, atmosphere.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

“If we just looked at Mercury with our own eyes, this would have been probably too thin to see. But look at it in the right wavelengths and, briefly, the planet might have been glowing,” says Prem.

While most of the atmosphere would have been quickly destroyed by powerful radiation from the sun, the researchers found that just over one-fifth of the water vapour from the impactor could have migrated to the poles and found shelter in permanently shadowed regions. This is more than many earlier calculations found, which better matches Messenger’s measurements, says Prem. A larger impactor coming in at a slower speed than has previously been suggested would be an even better match, trapping more water on the surface.

If the researchers are correct, all of this would have happened over the course of one Mercurian day, which is 176 Earth days. “This would certainly have been the most eventful day in the last billion years of Mercury’s history,” says Emily Costello at the University of Hawaiʻi.

This could answer the long-standing question of why Mercury has so much ice in its polar craters and Earth’s moon doesn’t, despite the two being remarkably similar in nearly every way. In short: “Mercury recently experienced a large-scale water delivery. The moon didn’t,” says Costello.

It could also help us figure out how and when the rest of the inner solar system, including Earth, got its water. “Mercury’s polar ice deposits are this interesting geological record of how and when water came to be in the inner solar system, and now we’re reading that record and trying to understand what it’s telling us,” says Prem. That mission will be helped along by the BepiColombo spacecraft, which launched in 2018 and will enter orbit around Mercury later this year.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how Mercury acquired its polar ice deposits through a large impact event, providing new insights into planetary science and the history of water in the inner solar system. This discovery has broad implications for space exploration and planetary formation theories, and it is well-supported by detailed research and simulations.
articles.personality_title: Mercury’s polar ice may have formed in a single day-long impact
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mercury is the closest planet to the sun and has very hot surface temperatures, yet thick ice deposits exist in permanently shadowed craters at its poles. Scientists have long wondered how this ice arrived and survived in such a harsh environment.

**What happened** – New research led by Parvathy Prem from Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory used computer simulations to show that about 100 million years ago, a large icy space object hit Mercury at a slower speed than previously thought. This impact created the Hokusai crater and vaporized much of the object, forming a thin, water-rich atmosphere. Some of this water vapor then moved to the cold, shadowed polar craters where it froze, creating the ice deposits.

**Impact** – This study offers the first detailed model of how Mercury’s ice formed in just one long Mercurian day (about 176 Earth days). It explains why Mercury has ice but Earth’s moon, which is similar in many ways, does not. The findings help scientists understand how water was delivered to planets in the inner solar system, including Earth.

**What’s next step** – The BepiColombo spacecraft, launched in 2018 and arriving at Mercury soon, will provide new data to test and improve these findings. Continued study of Mercury’s ice could reveal more about the history of water in our solar system.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single massive impact about 100 million years ago likely created Mercury’s polar ice deposits by delivering water that settled in permanently shadowed craters.
articles.personality_title_fr: La glace aux pôles de Mercure formée en un seul impact d’une journée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Mercure est la planète la plus proche du soleil avec des températures très élevées, mais elle possède malgré tout d’épais dépôts de glace dans des cratères en permanence à l’ombre à ses pôles. Les scientifiques cherchaient à comprendre comment cette glace a pu s’y installer.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude dirigée par Parvathy Prem du Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory a utilisé des simulations informatiques pour montrer qu’il y a environ 100 millions d’années, un gros objet glacé est entré en collision avec Mercure à une vitesse plus lente que prévu. L’impact a créé le cratère Hokusai et vaporisé une grande partie de l’objet, formant une atmosphère très fine riche en eau. Une partie de cette vapeur d’eau s’est déplacée vers les cratères froids et ombragés où elle s’est figée, créant les dépôts de glace.

**Impact** – Cette étude est la première à modéliser en détail comment la glace de Mercure s’est formée en une seule longue journée mercurienne (environ 176 jours terrestres). Elle explique pourquoi Mercure possède de la glace alors que la Lune, pourtant similaire, n’en a pas. Ces résultats aident à comprendre comment l’eau est arrivée sur les planètes du système solaire interne, y compris la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – La mission BepiColombo, lancée en 2018 et qui arrivera bientôt en orbite autour de Mercure, fournira de nouvelles données pour confirmer et améliorer ces découvertes. L’étude continue de la glace mercurienne pourrait révéler davantage sur l’histoire de l’eau dans notre système solaire.

**En une phrase** – Un impact massif unique il y a 100 millions d’années a probablement créé les glaces polaires de Mercure en apportant de l’eau qui s’est déposée dans des cratères en permanence à l’ombre.
articles.personality_title_es: El hielo polar de Mercurio pudo formarse en un solo impacto de un día
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Mercurio es el planeta más cercano al sol y tiene temperaturas muy altas, pero aún así posee gruesos depósitos de hielo en cráteres permanentemente sombreados en sus polos. Los científicos han querido entender cómo llegó ese hielo.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva investigación liderada por Parvathy Prem del Laboratorio de Física Aplicada Johns Hopkins usó simulaciones por computadora para mostrar que hace unos 100 millones de años, un gran objeto helado chocó contra Mercurio a una velocidad más lenta de lo que se pensaba. Este impacto creó el cráter Hokusai y vaporizaría gran parte del objeto, formando una atmósfera muy fina rica en agua. Parte de ese vapor de agua se trasladó a los cráteres fríos y sombreados, donde se congeló y formó los depósitos de hielo.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es el primero en modelar con detalle cómo se formó el hielo de Mercurio en un solo día mercuriano largo (alrededor de 176 días terrestres). Explica por qué Mercurio tiene hielo pero la Luna, que es similar, no. Los resultados ayudan a entender cómo el agua llegó a los planetas del sistema solar interior, incluida la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – La nave espacial BepiColombo, lanzada en 2018 y que llegará pronto a Mercurio, proporcionará nuevos datos para probar y mejorar estos hallazgos. Seguir estudiando el hielo de Mercurio podría revelar más sobre la historia del agua en nuestro sistema solar.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un solo gran impacto hace 100 millones de años probablemente creó los depósitos de hielo polar de Mercurio al entregar agua que se acumuló en cráteres permanentemente sombreados.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, massive icy impactor striking Mercury’s cratered surface, with ethereal, shimmering water vapor rising and drifting slowly toward the dark, permanently shadowed polar craters, capturing the moment of water delivery and ice formation under a soft, sunlit sky.

articles.title: See the clouds streaming and vanishing around this planet — 690 light years away
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 21 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01608-3James Webb Space Telescope reveals weather patterns from how planet WASP-94 A b filters the light of its parent star.
articles.content:
An artist’s representation of the exoplanet WASP-94 A b, showing clouds building up on the night side of the planet.Credit: Hannah Robbins, Johns Hopkins University

On the night side of a distant planet, thick clouds continuously form. But as winds carry them from the cool night side to the lit day side, the clouds quickly dissipate. Astronomers have been able to detect this weather pattern on the planet WASP-94 A b just by studying the light of its star coming from 210 parsecs (690 light years) away. The results show how tracking different phases of a planet’s rotation can can improve researchers’ understanding of planetary atmospheres, say the authors of the study published on 21 May in Science1.

Researchers can usually detect the presence of an extrasolar planet only indirectly — for instance, when the planet passes in front of its star, as viewed from Earth. Starlight filtered through a planet’s atmosphere can be analysed for clues about the planet’s chemistry, its weather patterns and even its origins. But even large planets block only 1% or so of the star’s light, and a much smaller amount of that light filters through the planet’s atmosphere. This leaves only feeble imprints on the star’s spectrum of light against a much brighter background.

JWST reveals first evidence of an exoplanet’s surprising chemistry

When Sagnick Mukherjee, an astronomer now at Arizona State University in Tempe, and his collaborators pointed the James Webb Space Telescope at the star WASP-94 A, they were in for a surprise. “We found this really amazing asymmetry in the data,” says Mukherjee. The telescope revealed tiny differences in the starlight’s spectrum between the start, middle and end of the planet’s transit in front of its star.

The measurements showed that during each transit, the part of the atmosphere that crosses first in front of the star is covered with thick clouds — probably made of droplets of minerals rather than water, given that the planet’s dayside temperature is at least 1,600 kelvin. But by the end of the transit, the trailing part of the atmosphere that crosses last is clear. Together, this suggests that as wind blows around the planet, clouds continuously form on the night side, cross into the day side and then dissipate.

Clear as day
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery using the James Webb Space Telescope, revealing detailed weather patterns on an exoplanet 690 light years away. This breakthrough enhances our understanding of planetary atmospheres beyond our solar system, representing a meaningful advancement in space science with broad implications for astronomy and planetary science.
articles.personality_title: James Webb Telescope reveals clouds forming and disappearing on a planet 690 light years away
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets, by watching how they affect the light from their stars. This helps them learn about the planet’s atmosphere and weather.

**What happened** – Using the James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers observed the planet WASP-94 A b, located 690 light years away. They noticed that thick clouds form on the planet’s dark night side. As winds carry these clouds to the bright day side, the clouds quickly vanish. This was discovered by studying changes in the star’s light as the planet moved in front of it.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have clearly seen weather patterns, like cloud formation and disappearance, on an exoplanet so far away. It helps us understand how atmospheres work on other worlds, especially those very different from Earth, such as planets with extremely hot days.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use this method to study other exoplanets and learn more about their atmospheres. This could reveal new information about planets’ climates and how they form.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The James Webb Space Telescope has detected clouds forming and vanishing on a distant planet, showing detailed weather patterns beyond our solar system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope James Webb révèle des nuages qui se forment et disparaissent sur une planète à 690 années-lumière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les planètes en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes, en observant comment elles modifient la lumière de leurs étoiles. Cela permet de mieux comprendre l’atmosphère et la météo de ces planètes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce au télescope spatial James Webb, des astronomes ont observé la planète WASP-94 A b, située à 690 années-lumière. Ils ont remarqué que de gros nuages se forment sur la face nocturne de la planète. Lorsque les vents les transportent vers la face éclairée, les nuages disparaissent rapidement. Cette découverte vient de l’étude des variations de la lumière de l’étoile quand la planète passe devant.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des scientifiques voient clairement des phénomènes météorologiques, comme la formation et la disparition de nuages, sur une exoplanète aussi lointaine. Cela aide à comprendre comment fonctionnent les atmosphères sur d’autres mondes, surtout ceux très différents de la Terre, comme des planètes très chaudes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront cette méthode pour étudier d’autres exoplanètes et en apprendre plus sur leurs atmosphères. Cela pourrait donner de nouvelles informations sur le climat et la formation des planètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le télescope James Webb a détecté des nuages qui se forment et disparaissent sur une planète lointaine, révélant des phénomènes météorologiques détaillés au-delà de notre système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: El telescopio James Webb revela nubes que se forman y desaparecen en un planeta a 690 años luz
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas, observando cómo afectan la luz de sus estrellas. Esto ayuda a entender la atmósfera y el clima de esos planetas.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el telescopio espacial James Webb, los astrónomos observaron el planeta WASP-94 A b, que está a 690 años luz. Notaron que en el lado oscuro de la planeta se forman nubes densas. Cuando el viento las lleva al lado iluminado, las nubes desaparecen rápidamente. Esto se descubrió al estudiar cambios en la luz de la estrella cuando el planeta pasaba frente a ella.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos ven claramente patrones climáticos, como la formación y desaparición de nubes, en un exoplaneta tan lejano. Esto ayuda a entender cómo funcionan las atmósferas en otros mundos, especialmente en planetas muy diferentes a la Tierra, como los que tienen días muy calientes.

**Qué sigue** – Los investigadores usarán esta técnica para estudiar otros exoplanetas y aprender más sobre sus atmósferas. Esto podría revelar nueva información sobre el clima y la formación de planetas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El telescopio James Webb detectó nubes que se forman y desaparecen en un planeta lejano, mostrando patrones climáticos detallados fuera de nuestro sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a distant, glowing exoplanet with a clear division between its dark night side, where thick, misty clouds swirl densely, and its bright day side, where the clouds dissolve into thin wisps carried away by gentle, flowing winds, all rendered in warm, soft natural tones against a deep starry background.
